speaker: interviewee
text: Carol, can I get up?

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Mr. Venezia, you were just telling us that the Italians came into your home. And--

speaker: interviewee
text: Not Italians.

speaker: interviewer
text: Excuse me. The-- the--

speaker: interviewee
text: The Greeks.

speaker: interviewer
text: The Greeks came into your home to take you away because they thought perhaps you were a spy.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you like to tell us more about that and explain what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was not only me. They took away all-- the most Italian citizens born in Greece. And they put 'em in a kind of a ghetto, call him, or whatever. No, they took us first, I remember, in a big building which was from a tobacco company, right on the rooftop. And then when the Italian came and start to bombard over there with the airplanes, they took us away from there because it was very dangerous. And they took us to another Italian school we had right by the ocean over there, which, over there, we got bombarded with the first day we were there. But nobody was hurt, anyway. And from there, in maybe in three or four days, they took us away from there and then they got us in Peloponnese, call him, which is at the end of Greece. And they put over there-- they put us over there in a-- in a soldier's camp, military camp. And then, because we had with us too many Italian citizens, Jewish, very rich-- was not only the Jewish, was case [INAUDIBLE] with us, all the Italians, they start to complain about it-- food, and for the beds, because they were very good-- very rich people. And they start to complain to the English Embassy, just to take us away from there because we're born in Greece. At least, to take off the Jewish only from there. Which they did. And they took us near Athens, to a very nice hotel. And all those rich people paying for our food and everything else from the restaurants around. Then, over there, we got liberated from the Germans. When the Germans came in Greece, they got-- we got liberated from the Germans. And right away, we went to the Jewish community. Because we don't know where to go. And the Jewish community was helping us and the Italian consulate was helping us too. And from there, we went to Thessaloniki, back again.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, let's go back a little bit.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because I want to understand exactly what happened. When the Greeks put you in kind of a ghetto, all together, who were you with? Who went with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Only me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know anyone else in the group?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Friend-- friend of mines. Because we were going to school together, and so on.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when they took you to Athens, to this hotel, when was that? Do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was about maybe a month later.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year?

speaker: interviewee
text: The year in 19-- I think when the-- the-- the war was declared.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old--

speaker: interviewee
text: December, in 1941.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I was about 20.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you in this hotel?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, we were in this hotel for about three months, I can say. That's all I remember. It was two or three months until the Germans came in. And we got liberated-- funny thing-- from the Germans. Isn't that funny?

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you being guarded while you were in this hotel?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they watching over you?

speaker: interviewee
text: We-- we was guarded.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was guarding you?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Greek policemans.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do the whole time you were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing. Just gaining weight from the good food we ate from the restaurants. The rich people was paying for us. So-- [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And when the Germans came in, how did they liberate you? What happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing. The Greek people, they let us free. And we went from Marousi, we went right in Athens. In Athens, we went right away to the Jewish community. And from there to the Italian consulate.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you in Athens altogether at that time? About how long?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, was not too long. Maybe a month. And then they send us to Thessaloniki. Because it was not too much-- it was-- were not too many ways to go to Thessaloniki at that time, because everything was destroyed, the trains and airplanes or ships. Was not so safe to travel at the time. So I think one month.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, while you were in Athens, what did you do? How did you spend your time?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Athens, what I was doing over there? Oh. We were sleeping in an hotel. And, with the money we had from the Jewish community or the Italian consulate, we were going-- we were going out at the restaurants to eat. And was-- was not much to eat anyway.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you part of any kind of a group or organization?

speaker: interviewee
text: No at that time, no. I didn't have no idea about those things.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you go back to Salonika?

speaker: interviewee
text: A month after.

speaker: interviewer
text: About what time of the year was it, do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, Carol. I cannot remember. I cannot remember. They came in in 1940, '41. I don't even remember what month the generals came in. I don't know. I don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when you returned, what were things like in Salonika when you came back?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they start to be a normal life again. My brother working as a barber and I start to work a little bit how, making a black market with the Italian people and with Germans, too. Just to be a help for my-- for my family. And always living [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] to be-- we were scared about the Germans all the time. And good thing that Thessaloniki was the most occupied, but from the Italian. Because the Italian people was much, much better than the Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: During that time, were you aware of any underground movements or any resistance movements that might have been going on?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not at all. But it was underground movement. But I didn't have no idea.

speaker: interviewer
text: How were the Germans in Salonika at that time? How did they treat you? Where were they? Where were they living? What were they doing?

speaker: interviewee
text: I can tell you they were animals. Hundred percent animals. We were afraid of them-- just to look at them. We didn't want to know nothing about them. We could stay away from them. I can say maybe the Wehrmacht, which was a regular army, they were not-- not so-- so savage like the SS mans. The SS mans was just-- not even look at 'em. They were-- they could kill you just for nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they causing problems at that time for the Jewish people in Salonika?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not at that time. Not at that time. Not after too long, they closed the [NON-ENGLISH] neighborhood and they make, over there, the ghetto. And it's too bad to say that-- was a Jewish guy which he knew German. And he became very close friend with the-- with the head of the-- of this ghetto, an officer, a-- another German officer, call him Vichencelli, which-- that Jewish guy was a spy for us. He was-- he was telling to-- to the Germans where we're living there-- Jewish people, the rich people, and-- and from the time, they start to get all the gold, all the money from the rich people and so on. And they made the first transport. Because like I said, was about 20, 50 meters from the-- from the ghetto was the train station. And they-- they took away the old neighborhood the-- the first time. And they put in the trains. When they return, were saying eight horses or 50-- 40 people. Those trains was only for horses. And they were putting all the sick people in there at the beginning, and then all the families, whatever they could. Filled up the trains, closed the doors. And in the back was a kind of a-- of the wagons-- was a kind of a-- a window, a little window. And they were putting some wires, those pointed wires, just to don't let-- not to let anybody get away from there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I was watching all those things. And then they were taking some other Jewish people from some other neighborhoods, especially the poor neighborhood. Because we had-- not all the Jews was rich. That's impossible. They-- they say now all the Jewish people are rich. That's just ridiculous. There were too many poor people over there, and working hard too. So they were taking the other people from the other neighborhoods and bringing them right-- right in the ghetto. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Which, this Jewish guy who was a traitor, he looked like he was God. He was thinking like he was God. Because he had everything over there. And then we had-- across the street of the synagogue was a kind of a hospital for the-- for crazy people. Over there, what he did, he got a friend of him, a big guy. And he was going to the Germans, where are the rich people? With their names, he knew their names, and so on.

speaker: interviewee
text: They came then to-- into the-- the ghetto. And bring him down to this hospital, down, very-- those rooms, for the crazy, they had. And his friend with a leather-- what do you call 'em, the--

speaker: interviewer
text: Whip?

speaker: interviewee
text: Whip. With a leather it, he was hitting all those Jewish guy to-- to-- to find out where they hide their gold. And this is the way this guy, Hason name, name of the-- this Jewish traitor, like I told you-- which, they killed him at the end, the Jewish guys killed him-- was taking all the-- all this gold from them. And this Hason gave him this gold to the-- to this guy, to Vichencelli.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember Hason's first name?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I don't remember his first name.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did he look like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Was a-- was a young guy. He-- not too tall, was almost short. Not-- well, a medium size. = had another brother in his family, which he sent them to the concentration camps in Poland. And he was, like I said, like a god over there. Going out with a single horse outside of the-- of the ghetto. Going out around Thessaloniki like he was a very rich guy and so on. So, well, I'm glad they killed him at the end.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you ever taken to this hospital for interrogation?

speaker: interviewee
text: Who? Me? No. The only thing my mother asked-- told me, that my grandmother [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] was in there. So with my [INAUDIBLE] certificate, I went in. And I-- when I found out my grandmother, she was in a single room of the crazy people. Because it was evacuated already. And when she saw me, she start crying, and said, Morrisico, Morrisico-- because Morris was my name-- Morrisico, it was [INAUDIBLE]. Take me away from here, crying.

speaker: interviewer
text: I said, don't worry. Where is your certificate, I told her. She said, this guy, this guy came in and took my certificate and he-- he threw it away. I said, what-- which guy? This-- this Hason. Don't worry, I said. I'll be here tomorrow and I'll get you out of here.

speaker: interviewee
text: I went to the Italian consulate and I get a new certificate for my grandmother. When the next day I went over there, that Hason send her away with the next transport. Can you imagine a Jewish guy, he could save-- he could save some of their lives. And he-- he send them-- he send them to the concentration camps.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was was when the Germans made the-- the ghetto for those transports to-- to [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember when the first transports went, what year it was?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was 1942. If I'm not mistaken. I-- I don't remember too many things anymore, after so many years, Carol. It's been already 50 years. And then I had-- I had so many things in my life, so many bad things in my life that happen, even over here. Not because of the United State, no. No. Because, like they say, God bless America. If we are alive, it's because of the American people, American soldiers, American military, which, they were so nice to us. They treat us so good at the liberation. Anyway, if it wasn't for them, we won't be alive. So many thousand of us.

speaker: interviewer
text: So going back to the time you went to take your grandmother out-- you came, and she was already gone.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then I went out with my paper, with my certificate, my Italian certificate. Because being an Italian citizen at that time, the Germans couldn't do nothing about it. But if-- if Hason would know it, maybe he will send me to the concentration camp right away, too. Because he didn't-- he didn't care.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you, at that time, able to do anything to help any of the other Greek Jews who didn't have Italian papers?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Italian consulate gives some certificates for some women that they were-- they were Italian citizens before. When they got married to a Greek husband, they became Greeks, too. So the Italian consulate, they didn't have nothing to do anymore with these kind of women. Because they were Greek citizens. But they still were giving-- the Italian consulate was still giving these Italian citizens certificate-- certificates, and named them Italians, just because they wanted to help.

speaker: interviewer
text: And can you tell us, how were the Germans treating the Greek citizens differently from the Italian citizens? Was there a difference? Or did they treat you, all Jews, the same?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Germans didn't know which was Italian citizen or not. Because we were speaking our language, a Greek language. Was no difference for them. Only when sometimes the German were stopping us for something, and I was showing them the Italian certificate-- like when I did-- when I went into the ghetto. To get out, I show them my Italian certificate, they let-- let me out. That was the only-- the only-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] the only reason, I mean, to let you out. If not, you couldn't go out.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, when the Germans made the ghetto, when they closed the ghetto, why were you going in and out? Did you have anyone inside?

speaker: interviewee
text: All my friends was in there, Carol. All my friends. I even sometimes take in a piece of bread, a kilo of bread, gave it to them. And we were scared all the time, because the Germans was-- German guards around. But many people could get away from there. Many people could get-- get away, easy, to get out of there and go to the-- to the partisans. Because at the time, I knew were some partisans. They could go and get away. But the Jewish people, the young people, they didn't want to go. Some of them, they went to the partisan. But the most of them, they didn't want to go. Because they wanted to go with their families. Because their families was old people. Father and mother, grandmother-- they were old people. And they were young. And they wanted to go, like they said in Poland, to go and work to hard labor only. Because we were Jewish. And those guys, they want to-- they didn't want to leave their family because they wanted to help the older people. That's the only reason they didn't escape.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, what did you know about the partisans at that time? What were they-- what did you know about them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, about the partisan, We didn't know so much, except that they blow up a train, or-- because, even-- even those guys, they were afraid to do many things. Because if they killed one German, the German was taking 20 or 50 guys from their prisons, Greeks. And they would shoot him.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see that happen?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. But we was reading on the paper, on the newspapers. Sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you ever involved with the underground or the partisans in any way?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not at that time. Only in Athens. Not in Thessaloniki. When they took us, when Italy capitulate, then the Italian consulate let us know that at that time, at that date, everybody who wants to leave Thessaloniki, to get a few belongings, whatever they can and to take him at the train station, so to take us to Athens. Because was almost all the transports was almost finished. Was about 55,000, 60,000 people, all the transports are almost finished.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the ghetto was empty. And when-- before the Italian capitulate, they took us to Athens. Because the Italian knew what was going to happen. Because if they-- they were not taking us to Athens, the German was taking us-- we were taken to the ghetto in Thessaloniki. That's why we walk with all the Italian citizens to Athens.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they took us to Athens in a-- in a Greek school, right on top of a little mountain over there, called [GREEK] this neighborhood. Which, we were sleeping in those rooms of the elementary school of that neighborhood. Where we were sleeping over there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who were you with?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was with my family, all my friends we knew at the-- all the Italian citizen, Jewish, at that time, only. And after-- after that, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] [SIGHS] I left over there. I gave 'em the-- I didn't give 'em the-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I didn't tell them where I was living and so on, and the address. I didn't give the address of that school to them. Because my brother and me, and Dario and his brother, we left the school and we were living in a places which this underground. The underground, calling EAM, which is the-- the part of the liberation of the Greek against the Germans. Because we became members of this underground-- what they call 'em-- these underground activities. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And we had the address of the school that we were living over there. Because we knew someday, they going to come-- the Germans are going to come and get us, all of us, from them, from the school. And we was living somewhere else. But every week, we were going-- every Friday, I remember-- we were going to go to the Jewish synagogue in Athens and to give a presence to the German, because we had a card over there, that we are present. Every week we supposed to go there. So, the underground over there, they was telling us to stay going over there. If anything happened, they going to help us not to let them-- the Germans to get us. That's why Dario and me and my brother, we were going every week to the synagogue-- just to have time, the rest of the week, to do something, to send, maybe, Jewish guy who needed to go to-- to send them to the partisans on the mountain, and so on and so on.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, tell us who-- tell us who Dario is. Explain to us.

speaker: interviewee
text: Who is Dario?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. His full name and who he is.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, Dario, his name is Dario-- or David, actually-- Gabbai. We two were friend, even with the family, we were friends and going to school together all the times. We knew-- we know each other since we were-- since our childhood. With Dario, we-- we-- our parents are like cousins, from his grandmother and my grandmother sisters.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, how did you become involved with the underground? How did you find out about them? Or did they find you?

speaker: interviewee
text: In every neighborhood was under-- was this kind of a collaborator they call him, or something of the young Greeks, which they didn't want the Germans. Because the Germans were killing, almost every day, Greek people. They didn't want to know about it. And they-- we became-- we had some relationship with the underground. Because many people of the underground were coming down in the city and talking to the head of this organization, calling EAM, to give some informations, what's going on, and so on, and what to do.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember who was in charge--

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: --Of this organization?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we never saw them.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were you-- what were your duties? What did you have to do for them exactly?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were going out almost every night, every night on the streets of Athens, with a bucket of red paint and a brush and marking down signs on the walls, on the sidewalks, saying-- at the, time I remember I was making a sign, 'Strong--' Strong, which was, I think, a German officer, which was too much against the Jewish-- 'leave the Jews alone. Hands down from the Jewish.' And almost every night, we were going around making those kind of signs.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then one night after the Italian capitulate-- did I say correct, capitulate? So in Athens, was an hospital, which over there, they were taking-- the Germans were taking Greek soldiers into the hospital, taking care of them. Because they were-- what they call 'em where they shoot a man? Wounded.

speaker: interviewee
text: Wounded. They were taking the Greek wounded people, take him to the hospital, make him well, and after that, take him where they were-- where they-- when-- they were-- they were not anymore sick, they were taken somewhere and shoot them. That was the law of the-- of the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, on one night, we knew that so many soldiers, the next day, they were going to get killed. And we had some arms from the Italians, when they capitulate, and some grenades. And they gave us instruction at that time, where there we were we going, in the side of the street or the other side of the street-- to [INAUDIBLE] the-- the hospital. And start like-- like a revolution or something, and to go into the hospital, which we did.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I remember, I threw a couple grenades myself. And we got-- I don't know how many soldiers we got out of that hospital so they won't get killed the next day. And I don't know how many guards of the Germans we killed in-- in that hospital.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a gun, too?

speaker: interviewee
text: Huh?

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you also have a gun, besides the grenades?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. At that night, we get only the grenades. I know, the other guys, maybe they had guns. I don't know. But my job was the grenades. They told me where, and how, and so on.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do with the soldiers when you got them out?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. Maybe they send them-- this organization-- maybe they send them to the mountains with the partisans.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're going to stop now. And we're going to change the tape--

speaker: interviewee
text: Fine.

speaker: interviewer
text: --and we'll start again.
